data.title: Garmin: A Strong Q1 To Start The Year But There Could Be Back-Half Headwinds
data.description:
Garmin (GRMN) had a solid print in Q1, which was released this week, and results exceeded on both the top and bottom lines. On the surface, the numbers were hard to argue with, with double-digit revenue growth and margin expansion. However, I think the current narrative around the stock may be a bit too optimistic given the setup heading into the back half of 2026.

Garmin's Q1 results were strong

For its Q1'26 results, Garmin reported a decent first quarter with revenues that came in at a record $1.75 billion, up 14% year over year, beating the Street's estimate of $1.72 billion (source: Bloomberg). Operating income reached $432 million, up 30% from the prior year quarter. On EPS, earnings per share of $2.08 came in well ahead of the $1.84 consensus. Overall, the stock was just up 2% on the day of the print.

Before getting into the details, it's worth understanding the basics of what Garmin does. The company operates across five segments: Fitness, Outdoor, Aviation, Marine, and Auto OEM. Each of those segments sells GPS-enabled hardware and related software into very different end markets with very different margin and growth profiles. That diversification is one of Garmin's strengths, but it also means you need to look underneath the consolidated numbers to understand what's really driving the business in any given quarter.

In its largest segment, Fitness, Garmin reported sales growth of 42% compared to last year, with strong demand for advanced wearables driving unit growth and management calling out "meaningful market share gains". For this segment, gross margins came in at 62% and operating margins at 29%, which are excellent numbers for a consumer hardware business. On the call, the company noted that they've been shifting from just GPS watches to higher-end, multi-sport watches through the Fenix and Forerunner series. A lot of growth has also come from capturing users who are graduating from basic "step counters" (like Apple Watch or entry-level Fitbits) to specialized devices that offer serious recovery and training metrics.

One of the questions I have, though, is what a 42% growth rate does to the comparables for Q1'27. Investors celebrating the fitness momentum today need to be thinking about what the segment looks like in 12 months when it's lapping this quarter. Management was careful to say fitness will be "the strongest contributor to 2026 consolidated growth," but that's a statement for this year, not a 2027 one. As I'll discuss in the valuation section. I think there's some risk the Street is extrapolating this segment's trajectory in a way that isn't fully warranted.

Aviation and Marine were both solid. Aviation revenue grew 18% to $264 million, with growth from both OEM and aftermarket. I think aviation is the most defensible segment Garmin operates. Switching costs are high, recertification cycles are long, and Garmin's position in general aviation is pretty unassailable.

In the marine business, revenues were up 11% to $355 million, but gross and operating margins came in at 56% and 26%, and management explicitly attributed the year-on-year margin compression to higher tariff costs. The marine segment is exposed to selling to people buying boats (which is a discretionary purchase), and the segment is already absorbing tariff pressure on margins. Despite margin contraction, operating income was still 4% higher than last year for the segment.

One of the weaker points in the portfolio was in the outdoor segment. Here, revenues fell 5% to $418 million, and management's explanation was that this quarter was compared against a strong prior-year period that included the launch of the Instinct 3 smartwatch family. That comp explanation isn't wrong, but I'd push back a little. Outdoor is Garmin's most product-cycle-dependent segment, and the fact that the current product lineup (which includes the well-reviewed Fenix watches) couldn't hold the segment flat on a year-over-year basis is worth noting. Management guided for Q2 outdoor performance to be "similar to Q1," meaning another consecutive quarter of softness, with recovery not expected until the back half of the year due to the timing of new product launches. I'll give management the benefit of the doubt on this for now, but it's an area I'll be watching closely in Q2.

Lastly, the auto OEM segment contributed $170 million of revenue and was up just 1%, with the segment posting a $6 million operating loss. For now, the operating losses are narrowing, and management noted that the Mercedes-Benz large-scale program should drive significant growth starting in 2027.

On the financials more broadly

Looking at the consolidated picture, operating income of $432 million on $1.75 billion of revenue gets you to a roughly 24.6% operating margin, which is up about 290 bps compared to last year. Adding back D&A on capital expenditures of $67 million gets you to roughly $500 million in quarterly EBITDA, which on an annualized basis implies an EBITDA run rate approaching $2 billion (consensus is at $2.28 billion for FY'26).

While I wouldn't necessarily call them red flags, there were certainly a few 'yellow flags' that I think the market may have missed. On the earnings call, CFO Doug Boessen noted that the 180 bps in gross margin was "primarily due to favorable foreign currency impacts" but there was no accompanying bridge or breakdown of how much impact it was attributable to.

EMEA revenue grew 15%, and APAC grew 25%, and both regions benefited from favorable FX. When you dig into that margin beat, it's likely a meaningful portion of it was currency and not structural improvement. So I think investors who are now modeling 59-plus percent gross margins for the full year should at minimum think through what happens if FX conditions normalize (i.e., the U.S. dollar strengthens) in the second half.

Secondly, as shown below, operating expenses grew 11% year over year, with R&D up $28 million and SG&A up about $31 million. With operating expenses roughly the same as a percentage of revenue for both of these line items, I was surprised to see little operating leverage here.

What's perhaps more interesting is on the inventory side. Inventory increased from $1.77 billion to $1.85 billion, which is something I'd keep an eye on. It increased both year over year and sequentially to approximately $1.9 billion. Management explained this as deliberate safety stocking in cost-sensitive components, with the note that rising input costs are not yet fully reflected in results but could create margin pressure beginning in 2027. I think that explanation is credible. But $1.9 billion in inventory against a quarterly revenue run rate of $1.75 billion is elevated, and if the outdoor back-half recovery underwhelms or fitness growth decelerates, that inventory becomes a different kind of conversation.

Outlook

Management sounded noticeably upbeat on the call and described registration rates as "some of the strongest that we've been experiencing in the near term" and characterized Garmin's customer base as "probably a little more resilient than what the average reporting out there is", citing the focus on active lifestyles and wellness consumers. I think that's reasonable. Someone who spends $700 on a Fenix watch is not the same consumer who's pulling back on discretionary spending because of tariff anxiety.

I also think the aviation growth narrative is credible, and I'd argue it's the most underappreciated part of the Garmin story. The segment's regulatory moats are real, and new certifications like the HondaJet Elite II Autoland approval expand the TAM over time. If Garmin can continue initiatives like these, they've got a good chance of consolidating the market and becoming the preferred provider.

Where I'm less convinced is the outdoor recovery thesis. Management is essentially asking investors to trust that product launches in the back half of the year will help a segment that's been soft for two consecutive quarters. In a consumer environment where spending is under some pressure, I think this will be an important part of the business to watch.

The other thing I noticed was that management maintained full-year guidance rather than raising it. Despite Garmin beating Q1 consensus by a meaningful margin on both revenue and earnings, the FY'26 guide stayed at $7.9 billion in revenue and $9.35 in EPS. Garmin typically runs conservative guidance, so I won't over-interpret this, but when you beat by as much as they did in Q1 and choose not to raise, I think it does signal that the back half of the year might be weaker. I think that would also explain why the stock isn't up more after the results.

What I'd expect in Q2 and the rest of 2026

For Q2, I think Fitness should remain strong even as it starts lapping elevated prior-year numbers. The weaker spots will be outdoor, marine, and auto OEM. My rough estimate is that Q2 will likely be tough to beat since there's a seasonal pattern where Q2 is historically the second-lowest quarter of the year. We haven't yet seen the sell-side model numbers lower on revenue, so it'll be interesting to see what happens.

The more interesting story for Garmin in 2026 is what happens in the second half of the year. If we assume the analyst estimates for Q2 numbers are accurate, the full-year guide of $7.9 billion implies roughly $4.4 billion in 2H'26 revenue against approximately $3.5 billion in the first half. That back-half weighting is meaningful, and it depends on a few things going right. I think investors will want to see the new Outdoor product launches resonating with consumers and fitness growth holding above 20% (showing that it wasn't just a short-term bump) even against tougher comparables. I think these two risks carry a fair bit of weight to the guide and are higher than what the current consensus implies.

Valuation

Garmin's valuation has rerated quite substantially since 2022, when it traded at around $80 per share at the low end and could be had for under 10x EV/EBITDA. Today, that valuation looks a lot more expensive at 3x the price and double the valuation.

At 19.0x EV/EBITDA and 26.2x P/E, Garmin trades expensively relative to peers that trade at 9.5x EV/EBITDA and 18.3x P/E (source: Bloomberg). While it's worth noting that the sell-side has modelled high single-digit EPS growth to the end of the decade for Garmin, I think it's a lofty valuation to pay for medium growth company.

Bottom line

Garmin is a great business that's well diversified across several end markets. With a solid balance sheet, high free cash flow conversion, and a fitness segment that continues to put up impressive numbers, the business has shown it has pricing power and that there is demand for its products. My 'hold' recommendation is really around the valuation. At roughly 26x forward earnings, I think the stock is pricing in a scenario where most of what needs to go right does go right. Fitness sustains elevated growth rates, outdoor recovers in the back half, tariffs don't worsen, and the US dollar doesn't strengthen are all being baked in, it seems. Any one of those assumptions coming in short of expectations, and I think the multiple has room to compress from here. For investors who like the long-term story, I'd look to add on a pullback toward the $210-220 range, where the risk/reward starts to look more balanced. Garmin is the kind of business I want to own; I just want a better price to buy it.

data.title: Middle East Disruptions Hitting Economic Activity In South Korea And Japan
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Korea: Output declines in refinery and semiconductor sectors

South Korea’s industrial production rose 0.3% month-on-month, seasonally adjusted, in March (vs 5.3% in February, 0.6% market consensus). Oil disruptions are one reason, but a technical payback after a strong gain in February probably contributed to the slowdown. Details showed that the robust output gain in transportation – cars (7.8%) and other transportation (12.3%) – offset the decline in semiconductors (-8.1%) and oil refineries (-6.3%). The production of hybrid vehicles and automotive components rose significantly. The output of container ships and aircraft parts also recorded notable growth.

We clearly see activity interruption in the petrochemical and refinery industry. Output (-6.3%), shipments (-5.4%). The utilisation rate for chemicals (-2.7%) and oil refineries (-5.2%) all dropped while inventories rose sharply 11.8%. We believe they have gone into survival mode – running the operation at a minimum level to avoid shutting down production entirely – as the supply situation worsens. We expect a sharp decline in oil supply for at least two months - in April and May - and that the weakness in refineries and related industries should intensify.

Strong chip demand will continue to boost economy

The drop in semiconductor activity was a surprise given the strong March export data. We believe seasonal adjustment could be a reason for the monthly fluctuation. The February gains were quite sharp. Thus, the March decline should be partly due to technical payback. Another reason could be that the strong chip performance is mostly driven by the price gain rather than the volume increase. Looking at the details, output dropped 8.1% (vs 28.2% in Feb), shipments also dropped 5.3% (vs 37.8%), and inventory dropped 19.2% after two months of gain. The utilisation rate also dropped quite sharply, 7.2%.

We don’t think the overall weakness was due to raw material supply issues. Instead, it seems chip manufacturers are adjusting production rates to suit their own interests. Today at Samsung Electronics’ first-quarter conference call, the company stated that while advancements in AI are increasing structural memory demand, expanding supply is likely to be limited because of the time required to build new capacity. Due to concerns about supply shortages, customers are already placing orders for 2027. Based on current orders, the gap between supply and demand in 2027 is expected to grow even larger than in 2026.

Tomorrow, Korea April trade data will be released. We expect exports to grow 50% YoY. Early April data shows a 183% growth in chips and a 48% rise in petrochemical exports. Refineries pass higher input costs through to output and tight supply of chips should push up prices.

We continue to believe that strong global chip demand will support overall Korean growth, outweighing the disruption of oil supplies if the energy situation follows ING's base case scenario.

Semiconductor output dropped but only for temporarily

GDP and BoK outlook

Going forward, the negative impact of energy disruption will increase in the current quarter. But we believe that semiconductors and other transportation activities, including vessels, should remain quite strong. Meanwhile, the government’s cash payout program has just begun. The lower-70%-household income group would receive up to a $400 consumption voucher. The supplementary budget also financially supports oil importers in importing non-Middle East oil and gas. Thus, the economy can avoid a contraction in the second quarter. Recent quarterly GDP growth of 1.7% was remarkable, but it should moderate sharply to 0.2% in 2Q26. We expect 2026 GDP to rise 2.8% year-on-year. Following ING’s oil scenario, we expect the supply to improve gradually in 2Q26. However, global energy prices are expected to remain above $90 by the end of the year.

The Bank of Korea will face difficult challenges. The strong growth rate is being driven mostly by external demand. The domestic economy is exposed to greater downside risks amid higher energy prices. However, as inflationary pressures broaden, the BoK is expected to deliver rate hikes, probably at a modest pace. We believe the BoK to hike in June and another one in 4Q26. The pace of rate hikes should be determined by developments in the Middle East and their impact on inflation.

Japan: Retail sales offset weaker-than-expected industrial production

Similar to Korea’s IP data today, Japanese industrial production was weaker than expected. IP unexpectedly dropped by 0.5% in March (vs. 1.1% market consensus), following a 2.0% drop in February. Output and shipments of chemicals, petroleum, and coal products fell, while electronic parts and devices increased.

However, the production forecast survey result showed that manufacturers expect an increase in output in April (2.1%) and May (2.2%). Thus, we expect the oil-related industry to weigh on growth in the current quarter, but gains in chips/equipment and transportation will cushion the shock.

Meanwhile, retail sales rose sharply by 1.3% MoM in March (vs -2.0% in February, 0.6% market consensus). Retail sales increased across all major categories. The significant rise in fuel consumption (8.2%) is likely due to consumers refuelling their tanks before prices rise. Thus, we may see a sharp decline in April. The monthly gain didn’t fully offset February’s decline. But the quarterly growth accelerated to 1.6% quarter-on-quarter compared to 0.6% in 4Q25; thus, it should still work favourably for 1Q26 growth.

Retail sales continue to be a driver of growth

Tokyo CPI is expected to rise firmly in April

Tomorrow, Tokyo CPI inflation data will be released. We expect it to rise to 1.7% YoY from 1.4% in March. Energy prices should rise modestly thanks to government subsidies. Amid strong wage growth, we expect businesses to pass on input cost hikes onto consumers, particularly since April is the usual month for the adjustment. Recent weakness in JPY should also add more pressure.

BoJ and market watch

Today's monthly activity data offers mixed signals for first-quarter growth. As the economy depends on domestic demand and services rather than exports and manufacturing, the slowdown in manufacturing should be offset by improving consumption. Thus, we expect first-quarter GDP to advance 0.3% QoQ, at a similar pace of 4Q25 growth.

Earlier this week, the Bank of Japan kept its policy rate steady at 0.75%. However, there is growing pressure for a rate increase amid rising inflation risks. We believe that oil disruptions will have a greater and longer-lasting effect on inflation than on economic growth. Therefore, we anticipate the BoJ will implement two rate hikes in the second half of 2026, with the first possibly happening as early as June.

As we mentioned in our earlier note, the BoJ's remaining on hold and a hawkish Federal Reserve put upside pressure on USDJPY. Intervention risks should limit the JPY depreciation to move not far above 160 level, yet it cannot be ruled out that the authorities may wait until the level reaches 165 to ensure that their intervention is effective. Pressure on Japanese government bonds (JGBs) intensified as well. We expect 10YJPY to continue to rise. We expect 10YJGB yields to rise to 2.75% by the end of 2026, along with a 50 bp hike by the BoJ. Ironically, the BoJ’s gradual hikes should put more pressure on long-maturity JGBs as markets now worry it's behind the curve and that inflation expectations may rise faster.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: China's PMI Data Suggests Domestic Demand Remains Soft
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

Official manufacturing PMI broadly stable in April

China's official manufacturing purchasing managers’ index edged down slightly to 50.3 in April from 50.4 but still came in stronger than both our forecast and broader market expectations.

In terms of the key subindices, production edged up 0.1pp to 51.5, while employment also moved 0.2pp higher, staying in contraction at 48.8. However, overall new orders dropped quite significantly to 50.6 from 51.6, suggesting weak orders domestically.

The more encouraging data points were the new export orders subindex, which rose 1.2pp to 50.3. It returned to expansionary levels for the first time since April 2024, as did the imports subindex, which eked back into expansionary territory at 50.1 for the first time since March 2024. This data suggests that trade activity remained solid in April.

In terms of price pressures, the raw material purchase price index (63.7) and ex-factory prices (55.1) both remained quite elevated, though down slightly from March's levels. This early data suggests that the reflation trend is continuing, likely to be confirmed by the inflation data out on 11 May.

The RatingDog manufacturing PMI also beat expectations solidly. It rose to 52.2, up from 50.8. The outperformance of this measure, which samples more export-oriented private firms, suggests that external demand continues to outpace domestic demand.

Export orders returned to expansion for the first time since 2024, but overall new orders slowed

Non-manufacturing PMI ties January for a 40-month low

Non-manufacturing PMI data was less encouraging in April, down to 49.4 again, tying January's data for a 40-month low.

The new orders subindex fell to 44.3, which was the lowest level since 2022. New export orders fared a little better at 47.3 but still remained in contraction for the 16th consecutive month. The only silver lining was a slight uptick in business expectations to 54.7, snapping a 3-month streak of deceleration.

Input prices moderated to 51.7 but remained above 50 for the sixth straight month. In contrast, price pressures haven't been transmitted to customers yet. The sales price component remained in contraction for the 31st consecutive month, coming in at 48.1.

As China's services sector tends to be more domestically oriented than manufacturing, it has started to underperform in recent months.

Non-manufacturing PMI has started to lag behind amid soft domestic demand

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Regency Centers: Fairly Valued Given Moderate Growth
data.description:
Shares of Regency Centers (REG) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 10%, a solid total return but below the market’s rally, given its relatively steady business. Even as long-term rates have remained high, Regency has pushed towards a 52-week high recently, reflecting the fact that grocery-anchored centers are among the most resilient asset classes in commercial real estate (“CRE”). However, Q1 results were basically in line, and shares traded modestly lower in late trading on Wednesday. I last covered shares in October, rating them a “Hold” in portfolios, but even a "Buy" was merited given the 12% rally since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Regency.

In the company’s first quarter, Regency Centers generated $1.20 of FFO (funds from operations), which was a penny below expectations. Regency in a grocery-anchored REIT. The grocery anchor helps to provide a steady flow of traffic, which then supports the other businesses. Importantly, it has an attractive tenant mix. Grocery, of course, is its largest component, at 20%. Plus, grocery stores have fairly limited e-commerce risk, adding to the durability of their foot traffic. I also view the fact that apparel is just 5% of the footprint.

Now, restaurant spending can be cyclical, which may be a source of concern, especially with higher oil prices. However, my analysis has found that restaurant spending is actually fairly resilient to gas prices. Many of its other categories, like banks, medical, and fitness, are resilient client types. Plus, it has brought down pharmacy exposure, an area of difficulty as operators close locations, to just 2%. This tenant mix should lead to strong occupancy, which is precisely what we are seeing.

Within its same property portfolio, 96.6% of space was leased, which is flat from last year. 94.3% is occupied, which is up 90 bps from last year. Generally speaking, REG and landlords do not begin earning rent until the lease has commenced and the space is officially occupied. It can take time after a tenant leaves a space for REG to make it ready for the next tenant, which is why rent is not earned yet. Narrowing this gap and commencing leases is a significant way REG will drive earnings growth this year.

This 230 bps gap between occupancy and leased rates is worth about $42 million of incremental revenue. Now, as you can see below, commencement rates are always below leased rates. That said, the gap is a bit wider than normal. While it has been narrowing, I view 150-175 bps as a normal gap, so we should see the commenced rate steadily pick up and potentially get to 95%, though that is likely as high as it goes. It will begin recognizing rent on 37% of its backlog by the end of this year.

Its anchor leased rate was 98.2%, a very healthy level. Beyond this, its shop lease rate is 94.1%, which is excellent. Small shops typically turn over more frequently than the anchors, leading to structurally lower occupancy. However, they pay substantially more in rent per square foot, as anchors get a discount for their size and the fact they help drive traffic to the center, which in turn supports the small shops. Getting this small shop occupancy up helps to boost margins. Frankly, I think small shop leasing is about as good as it can get.

While further occupancy gains from here are likely to be modest just given how high the level is, REG can still drive incremental revenue growth by raising rents when leases mature. During Q1, it signed 1.5 million square feet of leases with a blended cash spread of 12%. Given a lack of high-quality outdoor retail space and very limited construction levels, rents have been rising, and REG’s average lease is below market levels, given they rise just ~2% on average per year. As these roll over, REG can raise rates up to market levels to capture incremental revenue.

Thanks to the combined benefit of rising occupancy and increasing rental rates, same-property net operating income increased 4.4% from last year. Base rent growth contributed 3.6% of this growth, and the company also benefited from a decline in uncollectible rent. NOI margins compressed 60 bps to 68.5%, driving the slight FFO miss, as property taxes rose 8%, a bit more than I expected. I expect this pace of growth to slow and for margins to recover a bit.

Importantly, the company maintains a strong balance sheet, enabling this development activity. Net leverage ended the quarter at 4.9x on a consolidated basis and at 5.2x including pro-rata shares of unconsolidated ventures. This is well within its 5-5.5x target, and I believe the low 5x area is an appropriate area to operate. For the right acquisition, I would be comfortable with leverage temporarily in the upper-5x range. As you can see below, it has a series of maturities in coming years, and rolling over its debt will generally be done at higher interest rates, which will be a modest headwind for FFO growth.

The company has $635 million of ongoing redevelopment projects, which it expects to generate a 9% yield on, and with its balance sheet, REG is well positioned to fund these projects. Shares offer a 3.8% yield, and while it has a $500 million buyback authorization, I do not expect any material repurchases. REG should retain at least $345 million of cash after its dividend, making this payout highly secure. With this retained cash flow, REG can self-fund much of its cap-ex and M&A plans, and if anything, leverage may tick down slightly over the rest of the year.

Alongside results, Regency largely maintained guidance. In particular, it continues to guide to ~3.5% NOI growth and $4.83-$4.87 of FFO. It did, however, up its capital deployment plans, bumping redevelopment spending by $25 million and adding $25 million of net M&A. Given its recurring revenue streams and long-term leases, near-term cash flows should be highly predictable, and I generally do not view REG as a company likely to deviate materially from in-year guidance.

Back in October, I was looking for FFO to be about $4.83 this year, which is fairly similar to the current outlook. I am now looking for ~$4.85-$4.90, reflecting the continued strength in renewal rates. Shares are up double digits since I last covered Regency, but my FFO view is only ~1% higher. In other words, this rally has been driven more by multiple expansion than by fundamentals, which is a bit surprising given the elevated long-term rate environment, a challenge for real estate valuations and dividend stocks.

I still view REG as having ~3.5%-4.5% long-term growth, reflecting 2%-3% rent growth and a ~1.5% uplift from M&A and development, especially as the occupancy tailwind is basically complete. With a starting dividend yield of ~3.8%, that leaves shares with an ~8% long-term return opportunity. Frankly, I struggle to see much upside in valuation beyond this, especially with rates above 4% and just an in-line quarter. With solid fundamentals and a market-like return potential, I see no rush to sell shares, but going forward, I see REG as more an income than a capital appreciation stock. As such, I remain a "Hold" and would want a pullback to $70 before adding.

data.title: Fed's Holding Pattern Continues Amid Competing Risks
data.description:
Markets and observers weren’t surprised when the Federal Reserve held its policy rate steady at the April meeting. More notable, in our view, were the three dissents by voting participants who did not support keeping the implicit easing bias in the policy statement’s forward guidance language. We presume their preference would have been a stronger signal that the next interest rate move, whenever it occurs, could be either a hike or a cut.

Our view remains that the next move will be a rate cut, but the timing is far from clear.

Chair Jerome Powell’s press conference emphasized the wide range of possible outcomes associated with the Middle East conflict, along with generally high uncertainty. He suggested that changes to the statement were a close call – more committee members supported more hawkish changes than during the previous meeting in March.

Powell also argued that Fed policy is in a good place to react to the economic implications of the energy supply shock, which poses risks to both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate: maximum employment and price stability.

Markets thus far seem to have interpreted the Fed’s signals as a hawkish shift, though the reaction seems tempered by expectations that Kevin Warsh, the incoming Fed chair, will be able to keep the Fed on hold despite stagflationary pressures from the Middle East conflict.

We still think there is a high bar for the Fed to reverse course and hike rates. Given the significant uncertainty over energy prices and energy supply, the Fed is likely to hold rates steady until it sees the inflation/unemployment trade-off becoming clearer.

Why the tone skewed a bit more hawkish

With the Fed widely expected to be on hold, the meeting was always going to be about communications. The statement kept its implicit easing bias – which garnered the three dissents – while Governor Stephen Miran continued his pattern of dissenting in favor of easier policy.

The easing bias in the statement is subtle and centers around the forward guidance sentence: “In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate …” The word “additional” in this context has been interpreted to mean additional cuts.

The dissenters would have presumably preferred to more strongly suggest that the next rate move could be either a cut or a hike – we see a hawkish (or at least less dovish) leaning implied in their dissents. That forward guidance language could be changed or removed as soon as the next meeting, if developments warrant it.

At the press conference, Powell articulated a modest shift in Fed views in a more hawkish direction. In practice, this likely translates to a period of holding rates steady until the economic data, outlook, and balance of risks paint a clearer picture for the policy path.

Baseline still implies cuts, but timing looks more conditional

Despite all of this, we still expect the next rate move will eventually be a cut, and we still pinpoint roughly 3% as the neutral policy rate. However, the timing is uncertain. If the Iran conflict and energy shock appear more persistent, it could take longer for core inflation to more clearly begin to moderate back toward the Fed’s target, complicating the decision to ease monetary policy.

Many observers and policymakers remember the pain of the sharp inflation spike in 2021–2022. We see important differences between now and then that should help mitigate core goods price inflation spillovers into broader services categories.

The widespread inflation during the post-pandemic episode was also related to large fiscal transfers (such as federal spending packages to support households and businesses) and compounded by an extremely tight labor market – factors that aren’t present today.

Eventually, as energy prices moderate (assuming they do), the Fed could still cut a few more times to align the current policy range of 3.5%–3.75% with the Fed’s median estimate for neutral policy of roughly 3%.

On the other hand, in a risk scenario where there is a more prolonged disruption in physical energy supplies out of the Middle East, the trade-offs look starker.

Even though the U.S. is relatively insulated as a net energy exporter, the higher global recession risks, and likely tightening financial conditions, would eventually lead to rate cuts, in our view – although an initial surge in global inflation would likely delay the central bank’s reaction to weaker activity.

Warsh transition unlikely to shift policy outlook

This was likely Jerome Powell’s final meeting as Fed chair. He committed to staying on as a governor until the legal investigations into him and the Fed building renovation costs were over with “transparency and finality.” He also said that Fed officials should be able to “make monetary policy without political considerations.”

He did commit to stepping down, but his comments leave plenty of room for interpretation on when he might feel comfortable with leaving. He also committed to maintaining a “low profile” as a governor and to aiding soon-to-be Chair Warsh where he can.

Powell also shared that Fed officials are worried about a continuation of challenges against the Fed or its people, and he specifically noted that the removal of Federal Reserve Bank presidents due to policy choices would be “the beginning of the end.”

In our view, the Warsh transition should mainly affect Fed communication – and market interpretation – rather than interest rates themselves. Warsh did not seem overtly dovish in his Senate hearing. Indeed, he criticized the Fed for being late to act on inflation in 2022.

He did point to trimmed mean and median inflation measures, which are currently running under core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) inflation – a modestly dovish lean. These measures have historically tended to run above core inflation and will likely accelerate if higher energy prices broadly push core goods inflation higher.

Our working assumption remains that Warsh’s current bias (similar to the Fed’s median) is toward cuts and that the Fed will remain an independent institution under his watch.

Disclosures

All investments contain risk and may lose value.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. data.title: Cognex: Reindustrialization And The Military Industrial Complex Can Drive Global Growth
data.description:
Cognex Corporation (CGNX) is set to report Q1'26 earnings on May 6, 2026, after market close, with significant analyst optimism going into earnings with 14 upward revisions and 0 downward revisions to EPS estimates in the last 90 days. With a growing market opportunity for machine vision underpinned by increased manufacturing productivity and autonomous systems, I have reason to believe that Cognex will be a major participant in the reindustrialization of North America, delivering strong growth across small- to large-scale manufacturers. With a robust start to the year in the manufacturing sector, I am more optimistic about Cognex's growth trajectory for eFY26, estimating earnings to come in above the midpoint guidance of $0.24/share; I am recommending CGNX shares with a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $80/share at 30.84x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Cognex Operational Outlook and Market Opportunity

The ISM-PMI Manufacturing Index has printed 3 consecutive months in expansionary territory, starting 2026 with strong footing. Core indicators from the index include robust new orders, production, supplier deliveries, and backlog of orders, all exhibiting strong expansions in recent months, suggesting durability in growth in the manufacturing sector. Offsetting these figures include employment and inventories, which may suggest productivity is up while potentially leveraging more autonomous systems to enhance productivity. A major offset is price inflation, which printed a significant expansion at 78.3% in March 2026, telling me that manufacturers may need to focus on driving down costs in order to maintain profitability. This can be a good sign for Cognex for its inspection tools, automating a critical component of the manufacturing process to ensure quality production.

At the global scale, industrial manufacturing was forecast for modest growth for 2026, which can potentially shift over the coming months as this report pre-dates the start of the war in Iran. One of the sticking points in global production involves elevated costs, which may add pressure with international oil and gas prices remaining elevated due to supply constraints coming out of the Middle East, which has effectively taken offline 10% of the world's oil and 20% of the natural gas supply, potentially adding cost constraints to European and Asian economies.

A major shift that can be supportive of Cognex's international efforts is military mobilization in Europe, where automotive OEMs are repurposing facilities to support militarization production, potentially acting as a catalyst for increased investment in shifting production lines and modernizing manufacturing. This could potentially deliver growth for Cognex for its machine vision technology designed to be implemented and scaled with minimal pretraining efforts, particularly with its latest AI-enabled visual scanning technology.

A similar phenomenon is occurring in the US, with the Pentagon approaching automotive OEMs and manufacturers to help in military mobilization efforts. For both Europe and the US, this transition is largely underpinned by depleted resources since the start of the Ukrainian/Russian war, where munitions were largely depleted. This may mean increased investments in domestic productivity, shifting supply lines, and increased investments in autonomous technologies over the coming years. For example, Boeing (BA) recently received a 7-year framework to expand PAC-3 interceptors under the pretense of tripling production. Lockheed Martin (LMT) recently announced a $4.7b contract to accelerate its expansion efforts of PAC-3 production. While much of Cognex's business resides in the logistics and warehousing markets, the growing military mobilization efforts could drive growth for the firm, expanding its presence in aerospace & defense.

Another major growth opportunity is in the semiconductor fabrication market. With semiconductor sales estimated to grow to $1.6T by 2030, Cognex may be in an appealing position to deliver its machine vision for semiconductor testing as the chip fabrication footprint expands. Much of the growth is expected to derive from memory chips as a critical component of advanced GPUs and TPUs. Supporting this growth are megadeals for multiple gigawatts of capacity to be delivered over the coming years. For example, Broadcom (AVGO) recently signed an agreement to deliver 3.5 GW of Alphabet (GOOG) TPU compute capacity as part of a multi-year rollout. Meta Platforms (META) recently signed an agreement to deploy Amazon (AMZN) AWS Graviton processors at scale. Intel Corp. (INTC) recently expanded its partnership with Alphabet to deliver Xeon 6 CPUs as well as a collaboration to develop IPUs. Intel also announced an agreement with Tesla (TSLA) to develop its Terafab, a program that is expected to produce 1 mm chips per month when fully scaled to support SpaceX/xAI and Tesla's autonomous efforts. Overall, the semiconductor market is ripe with growth that may deliver improved economics for Cognex's machine vision technology for inspection.

Overall, I'm optimistic about Cognex's positioning in the market. With multiple growth trends to participate in, Cognex should realize durable, margin-accretive growth over the coming years that has the ability to drive share value.

Cognex Financial Forecast

Looking ahead to EQ1'26, I'm forecasting Cognex to deliver $255 million in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.26/share. While my forecast is at the top end of management's guidance, I believe that the market signals provide support for elevated growth for the company. With the expectation of increasing manufacturing productivity in the US and Europe as manufacturing is reshored and repurposed for militarization, I believe there may be a greater opportunity in the market to deliver stronger growth.

Risks Related to Cognex

Bull Case

Global reindustrialization and manufacturing reshoring can deliver strong tailwinds, particularly during an inflationary market. Facility retooling for militarization can also be a potential growth driver as production lines are repurposed for different components. Increasing investments for data center capacity can deliver more investments in chip manufacturing and inspection.

Bear Case

Cognex has short, 90-day lead times and has limited visibility beyond a single quarter. High energy prices could curtail manufacturing across Europe and Asia and may slow growth in these regions for Cognex.

Valuation and Shareholder Value

CGNX shares are currently trading below their historical midpoint trading range at 40.49x EV/aEBITDA, creating an appealing investment opportunity going into earnings. With Cognex's long-term margin-accretive growth trajectory, I believe shares can grow alongside operational performance.

Fundamentally, I believe shares should be priced at $80/share at 30.84x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for CGNX shares.

Using the model, the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm's "financial position" section and ties it to the stock's historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock's peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company's stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: BWX Technologies: Great Company But Priced To Perfection
data.description:
BWX Technologies (BWXT) creates nuclear solutions primarily for the US Navy. The company has a highly differentiated manufacturing process and competitive moat. The stock has been on a great run YTD, up nearly 21%, but with the multiple expansion having been so aggressive in the past few years, the stock is priced to perfection, and there is effectively little room for upside for prospective investors. The fundamentals are very strong and rather supportive of the company's operations, but investors should look for a better entry point to get long on this one.

Business Profile

BWX Technologies is actually a spin-out from Babcock & Wilcox in 2015, where the former company separated its nuclear innovation business and its power generation business.

The company has two primary operating segments: Government Operations ("GO") and Commercial Operations ("CO"). The GO segment is the larger of the two, accounting for essentially two-thirds of the company's revenue and EBITDA. They manufacture highly engineered naval nuclear propulsion components, as well as specialty materials and space and defense microreactors. The company also provides technical services for these products.

Especially on the GO side of the business, it cannot be understated the competitive positioning of the company. For example, they are the owner and operator of the only two NRC Category 1-licensed nuclear facilities that can handle, store, and process highly enriched uranium in the US. Additionally, the company is a "sole source" supplier. This essentially means that products are built to spec for a given program such that the contract supplier is locked in. This happens when a company wants certainty that parts or full products will be produced with precision. A good example of this elsewhere is actually with RBC Bearings (RBC) being the sole-source supplier on certain highly specialized bearings for the Boeing (BA) 737 program. These are often multi-year preceding timelines for component and product design that enforce this competitive moat, and it's done to ensure the absolute highest quality final product. For investors, this also means long-term visibility on revenue and cash flow.

On the CO side of the business, the company manufactures nuclear power-related engineered components and uranium fuel, as well as providing life-of-plant services. This segment is also home to nuclear medicine, where the company produces radioisotopes. End markets can include things like clean energy, pharmaceuticals, and industrial companies. Some examples of customers are Boston Scientific, Bayer, TerraPower, and GE. The CO side of the business is geared towards structural megatrends like increased power consumption, decarbonization, and the appetite for nuclear technology.

The company is perhaps the most levered way to play an increasing defense department budget. Simply put, more defense spending equates to more potential programs that BWXT can service and, therefore, better financial results. Additionally, the more robust shipbuilding programs are for the US Navy, the better BWXT can financially perform.

Financial Profile and Forward Estimates

The company does have a long-term algorithm for mid- to high-single-digit EBITDA growth and double-digit free cash flow growth over the medium term. This was presented back in 2024 at the company's Investor Day, seen below.

As we turn to the year ahead, the company has provided guidance.

  • Revenue: $3.75 billion
  • Adj. EBITDA $645-$660 million
  • Adj. EPS $4.55-$4.70
  • FCF $305-$320 million

This guidance is robust and well above algorithm. Revenue is growing nearly 17% in this annual guide, while EBITDA is growing 13.5%, effectively double the company's algorithm.

Q1 earnings will be on May 4 next week, so I wanted to revisit estimates to see how that will go. The GO segment was up 14% in Q1 last year, while CO was up 10% for consolidated revenue growth of 13%. I am modeling 20% GO growth this quarter and 40% CO growth, for consolidated revenue of $846 million versus the street at $838.6 million. Q4 GO contracted but was largely a function of timing within 2025, while CO outperformed, also as a function of timing. Still, organic growth was 31% in Q4, which creates clear momentum for Q1.

I am assuming 50 bps of margin expansion at the operating level to get to $135 million in adj. operating income. After factoring in an 18% tax rate and about $4.5 million for the combination of interest and other expenses, the model shakes out to $1.17 in EPS versus the street at $0.93. This should handily beat it. Since it is early in the year, I would expect a reaffirmation of the guide, but likely a flow-through of the earnings beat to adj. EPS for an effective raise of 5.1% at the midpoint.

Appropriately Rich Valuation, But Little Upside

Just to focus on the free cash flow, if we were to assume they hit the midpoint of their guidance, the company would be doing approximately $312 million in free cash flow. The market cap at the time of writing is approximately $20.35 billion, which means the yield is approximately 1.5%. This leaves very little room for upside, in my opinion. Free cash flow yield can be a very helpful metric in identifying how much room the multiple has to run, and at 1.5%, this is trading in the upper decile.

One would need to make the case that free cash flow is going to end up being ~$500-$600 million inside 12 months and grow to $1 billion by the end of a 2- or 3-year period to justify entry here. This isn't to say the company doesn't have exciting growth opportunities. Quite the contrary, actually. There are opportunities in microreactors, specialty materials processing expansion, more shipbuilding with the US government at better prices, etc. All of these are excellent opportunities for the company; they just seem to be largely reflected in consensus estimates.

Additionally, free cash flow conversion is already high. If you take the midpoint of the adj. EPS guidance and back into implied adjusted net income, it's approximately $424 million. This would imply that the midpoint free cash flow conversion figure is nearly 73.5%. It could improve to 90%+, which is the hallmark of exceptional companies, but that process usually takes several years for any well-run company. Even then, at a 90% free cash flow conversion rate, the free cash flow yield would be approximately 1.8%, which still leaves little room for multiple expansion.

The stock has been a monster over the last five years, with returns compounding year after year. This rise has carried with it several turns of multiple expansion, and while not unfounded, as the company has been putting up stellar results, it has left the stock trading at an unsustainable valuation.

If we try to incorporate relative valuation, I'm going to use the peer set of Huntington Ingalls (HII), General Dynamics (GD), Northrop Grumman (NOC), Leidos (LDOS), and AECOM (ACM).

  • BWXT trades at 49x forward earnings with a 1.5% free cash flow yield
  • HII trades at 20.6x forward earnings with a 3.8% free cash flow yield
  • GD trades at 19.2x forward earnings with a 3.9% free cash flow yield
  • NOC trades at 20.6x forward earnings with a 3.4% free cash flow yield
  • LDOS trades at 12.8x forward earnings with an 8.8% free cash flow yield
  • ACM trades at 13.8x forward earnings with a 6.8% free cash flow yield

BWXT's valuation is quite simply extended on a multiple basis and on a free cash flow basis. It is difficult to justify entry here. I like the stock, the growth prospects, and the fundamental operating environment for the company, but the valuation prices all of this in.

Risks

There are multiple risks of investing in BWXT, aside from the high valuation.

First, nuclear risk and associated safety risk are critically important. Even a small incident like a plant shutdown can cause material financial impacts, and in the ultra-rare case where there is a safety incident, BWXT could pose huge risks to operating personnel, and the company could lose its licenses. Effectively, there is a zero-tolerance margin with a name like this.

Second, given the concentration of revenue to the US government, there is significant risk in the sense that the budget for shipbuilding needs to continue to grow, broader defense spending needs to grow, and the administration needs to be supportive of BWXT from a license standpoint. This includes not seeking out lower bids for future projects and granting licenses to another company. Now, this is a very high-barrier-to-entry industry that cannot be replicated easily, so my thought is this risk is not something the investment community needs to worry about on a day-to-day basis.

Rather, the perception of future funding and budget growth is really where investors should be focused. That means political risk is a key factor to consider with BWXT. We are in a midterm election year, so the elections in November could change the outlook for defense spending in the event of Democrats winning either the House and/or Senate.

Conclusion

BWX Technologies has decades of experience in nuclear operations, and with great revenue visibility, the company is one of the few out there that investors can feel confident will be able to hit their targets. With that being said, the valuation fully reflects this, and I see little room for multiple expansion. Absent a brand new program being awarded to the company tomorrow, assuming they would even have the capacity to ramp into it, there's little in the way of justifying the level it is trading at. I would look for opportunities elsewhere in the space.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Spikes With No U.S.-Iran Resolution In Sight
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy – Reality sets in

The oil market has moved from over-optimism to the reality of the supply disruption we are seeing in the Persian Gulf. ICE Brent traded above $122/bbl yesterday, its highest levels since the start of the Iran war. The breakdown of talks between the US and Iran, along with President Trump reportedly rejecting Iran’s proposal for a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, has the market losing hope for any quick resumption in oil flows.

While we estimate demand destruction in the region of 1.6m b/d, which is significant, it’s clearly not enough to fill the supply gap we are currently facing. The longer this disruption persists, the less the market can rely on inventory, and the greater the need for further demand destruction. The only way to drive this would be through higher oil prices.

Energy Information Administration (EIA) data continues to show buyers turning to the US for alternative supplies amid the ongoing Persian Gulf disruptions. US crude oil exports jumped 1.64m b/d WoW to 6.44m b/d over the last week. This is a record high, surpassing the previous record of 5.63m b/d in February 2023.

Total oil and refined product exports hit a record 14.18m b/d over the week, marking the fifth consecutive week of record export volumes. These strong export volumes are increasingly tightening up the US domestic market, with commercial crude oil inventories falling by 6.23m barrels over the week.

When you factor in releases from the strategic petroleum reserve, total crude inventories declined by 13.36m barrels. For now, implied demand data shows that US oil demand is holding up well despite higher prices. Total implied oil demand rose 1.43m b/d week-on-week.

While the oil market is increasingly accepting the reality of ongoing supply disruptions, the gas market has been more measured. While European gas prices have moved higher in recent days, with TTF still trading below EUR50/MWh, they don’t appear to fully reflect the scale of LNG disruptions.

The seasonally weaker demand period we are currently in will provide some comfort. There has already been a fair amount of demand destruction in the LNG market, which will help to take some pressure off prices. Clearly, the longer this persists, the tougher the job Europe will face refilling storage ahead of the 2026/27 winter.

For now, EU gas storage continues to tick higher, with it a little more than 32% full, while LNG send-outs remain at seasonally high levels. These strong send-outs won’t continue if supply disruptions persist, particularly with JKM trading at a premium to TTF.

The latest positioning data shows that investment funds increased their net long in TTF by 3.37TWh to 263.6 TWh over the last reporting week. The gross long position remains some distance from the peak seen in January, suggesting funds still have further room to increase their positioning.

Metals - Copper steady, gold extends losses

Copper held near recent highs, supported by pre-holiday restocking in China, which is offsetting macro and geopolitical uncertainty. Buying ahead of the Labour Day holiday has provided near-term support following the recent pullback.

The focus remains on the Iran conflict and potential implications for global growth. In particular, risks to energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz continue to cap upside. In China, stricter enforcement around invoicing may weigh on spot activity and slow inventory draws. Supply-side risks linked to war-related disruptions, especially constraints on inputs such as sulphur, continue to underpin the market.

In precious metals, gold edged lower, extending recent losses. Market attention remains on diplomatic efforts and US enforcement around Iranian ports. This is keeping energy-related inflation risks elevated but limiting support for non-yielding assets.

Firmer energy prices are reinforcing expectations that central banks may keep policy restrictive for longer, pushing real yields higher and weighing on bullion. Near-term price action remains sensitive to developments around Hormuz, with any de-escalation likely to cap upside.

Agriculture – EU weighs suspension of duty-free sugar imports

The European Commission plans a temporary halt to the EU’s inward sugar processing scheme, citing concerns that rules allowing duty-free sugar imports for re-export after processing are being misused. Member states are scheduled to vote Thursday on a one-year pause.

Beet farmers and processors point to a discrepancy between roughly 700kt of sugar imported under the scheme and the smaller volumes later exported. They argue that freezing these imports would help support prices and ease pressure on beet growers.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Power Integrations: Drivers Sending The Stock Vertical Do Not Look Sturdy Enough
data.description:
Power Integrations (POWI), a supplier of high-voltage power conversion solutions for the semiconductor industry, was essentially stuck with the stock basically going sideways for eight months or so. That changed in April when POWI went pretty much vertical by gaining as much as 48.9% at one point. However, the foundation supporting this large and rapid appreciation in the stock price does not appear to be very solid. It seems to rely rather extensively on assumptions, especially as it relates to future growth, something that could become an issue with the next earnings report due on May 7.

POWI comes back to life in a big way

In my September 2025 article about POWI, I mentioned how POWI’s decision to essentially make a fresh start with a new CEO was probably unavoidable with the way the company had underperformed for years. The company had, for instance, made a number of what eventually turned out to be overly optimistic growth projections into the future by prematurely calling for a recovery in demand, which it was forced to subsequently retract as it became clear demand would not be as strong. This change at the helm could conceivably result in POWI doing better, but I also pointed out how the stock remained in a downtrend, which went back several years. Accordingly, I decided to rate POWI a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how the prior rating proved to be on point for months with the stock basically going sideways, mostly in the forties, which is where the stock traded when the aforementioned article was written. Notice, for instance, how the stock stayed below the $50 price point, starting in August, for well over half a year, as shown in the chart.

This stayed true up to and including March, before POWI made its way into the fifties, which paved the way for a powerful rally in April that went pretty much vertical at one point. This resulted in POWI breaking through the long-term trendline mentioned in the previous article. The chart below shows how POWI accelerated once it managed to get past the descending trendline that had kept it contained for years.

It’s worth mentioning that POWI was not the only semi that went into overdrive in April. The whole sector rallied in April, which appeared to have rubbed off on POWI as it got a lift from semis going higher. Capital flowed into semiconductors, in large part due to AI, and POWI seems to have benefited from this dynamic as a semiconductor stock.

As long as the aforementioned trendline was in place, a strong argument against long POWI existed, but with the trend broken, this argument is no longer applicable. This very likely assisted with the recent acceleration in the stock price. On the other hand, the surge caused the stock to become very overbought, which likely played a role in causing the stock to pull back in the last few days. Some short covering may also have played a role.

Why the upcoming report could become a catalyst

Another reason that likely played a part is that valuations had become so elevated, which remains true even with the stock off the highs. For instance, POWI has a P/E ratio of 52.8x with TTM non-GAAP EPS of $1.25, and the stock last closed at $66.02, which is not only more than double the median in the sector but also well above the low thirties POWI averaged in the past five years.

Keep in mind that this is with the stock ceding a portion of the recent gains. POWI is still up 85.8% YTD after gaining as much as 114.4%, which gives one a sense of how elevated valuations got during the April spike. These elevated valuations likely convinced some to lower their exposure after the recent surge in the stock price, which contributed to the stock retreating in the past few days.

Another issue likely on people’s minds is the upcoming Q1 FY2026 report, which is scheduled to be released on May 7. My estimate is that POWI will announce Q1 FY2026 non-GAAP EPS of $0.24 on revenue of $106.5M, or somewhat better than the $0.23 POWI earned on revenue of $103.2M in the preceding Q4 FY2025 report.

Yet both would be worse than what POWI achieved a year before when it earned $0.31 in Q1 FY2025 and $0.30 in Q4 FY2024. In fact, it is worth mentioning that although the stock price has soared higher, quarterly results have not improved anywhere as much. The chart posted earlier shows how the stock price fell in 2023, and this had everything to do with the downturn in demand that caused sales and profits to contract.

For instance, GAAP EPS shrank in each of the past three completed fiscal years, or FY2023-2025, after going from $2.93 in FY2022 to just $0.39 in FY2025, as announced in the Form 10-K released in February. In terms of non-GAAP, non-GAAP EPS fell from $3.29 in FY2022 to $1.25 in FY2025, although this was better than the $1.16 of FY2024. FY2025 revenue grew by 6% YoY to $443.5M. The balance sheet is also fine with just $19M in debt, more than offset by cash, cash equivalents, and short-term marketable securities totaling $249.5M.

POWI has not done all that much recently to support a rally in the stock price as large as the recent one, which is expected to continue in the short term with the consensus estimate for FY2026 non-GAAP EPS at $1.28, or just $0.03 better than FY2025. These estimates are a reflection of POWI’s own outlook, which calls for modest levels of growth in FY2026, similar to FY2025. From the Q4 FY2025 earnings call:

“I think for '26, we're planning on, I'll say, similar growth levels year-over-year. As Jen said, though, it is very early in the year, and we're going to have to see how demand plays out in the first half. It has been lumpy in certain areas to date. But net-net, we're going to be planning for similar growth. However, I will say we're going to be cautious in our investments until we see those bookings really taking form and the step-ups that we expect to see, making sure that they are happening before we dive in deeper to certain investment areas. So you're going to see us be cautious on that as we are in '26. But we are optimistic over -- as Jen said, over the next couple of years, the markets that we're entering have great opportunity for us to step up that growth rate in the outer years.”

This soft growth outlook helps explain why the Q4 FY2025 report was essentially a nothingburger in terms of moving the stock when it was released in February, as shown in the chart posted earlier, which shows how the stock continued the flattish, sideways action after the report’s release that it exhibited before the report’s release. However, it needs reminding that POWI did leave open the possibility the outlook could be revised higher as FY2026 goes by.

POWI also alluded to faster growth in the coming years, even if it did not provide any numbers, and these are those who believe POWI will do better down the line. For instance, Wall Street earnings estimates for FY2027 range from $1.60 at the low end to the most optimistic estimate of $2.31 at the high end, which implies YoY increases of 25.0% and 80.5%, respectively, using $1.28 in FY2026 as the base.

The next earnings call could be significant in that investors will be looking to see if POWI will revise its initial outlook to something with stronger growth. Any clues that growth at POWI will accelerate to the point that it can begin to recoup the substantial ground it lost in terms of revenue and earnings since the highs of FY2022 could serve to give the recent rally another lease on life.

On the other hand, if the outlook remains tepid, like the last report, or worse, becomes weaker, the stock could get pummeled, especially after the recent spike, because it would essentially imply the rally in the stock price was undeserved. While current FY2027 estimates call for stronger growth, there is nothing stopping them from being revised downwards.

Keep in mind POWI never actually stated growth would be anywhere as strong. It also never explicitly said it would happen in 2027. POWI simply suggested growth could step up from the current levels of growth in the mid-single digits or so, which could just as well mean high single digits instead of the mid-single digits, but well below even the most conservative of Wall Street estimates.

How POWI could try to boost growth

As shown earlier, POWI has been rather lackluster in recent years when it comes to growing the top and the bottom line. On the other hand, the stock price has soared higher, and if POWI is to justify the much higher valuations that came with it, POWI will need to boost growth, or the stock price will not be able to stay aloft for very long.

This growth does not have to come right away, but there has to be stronger growth ahead, or one can legitimately make an argument that POWI is overvalued. For instance, according to the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch, POWI, trading at a P/E ratio of 52.8x, needs to grow non-GAAP EPS at a CAGR of 52.8% in the next 3-5 years in order to be considered fairly valued relative to growth.

POWI has not called for growth anywhere as fast. It is also much faster than the growth POWI was able to achieve in recent years. However, the market may be willing to settle for something less in the short term if POWI can show just enough to think that faster growth is likely further down the line. Many semis have been able to achieve this in recent times by fetching valuations that seem much higher relative to their growth because of the perception that growth could accelerate further, usually due to AI.

POWI could attempt something similar by attempting to duplicate the growth potential inherent to AI. POWI has not had much exposure to AI since it is traditionally more focused on the consumer side with consumer products like household appliances and smartphone chargers, but the new CEO did mention after taking office that POWI would attempt to increase its exposure to data centers by developing products for them. For example, potential customers may be interested in InnoSwitch for auxiliary power in datacenters.

This particular InnoSwitch is made using gallium nitride, or GaN, which has the potential to result in higher efficiency and thus lower cost. This could be useful for data center applications since they tend to consume a lot of electric power. POWI has really focused on incorporating GaN in its products in recent years, which is where POWI can try to make inroads in the data center market. GaN could make a difference for POWI.

Could POWI pull a rabbit out of a hat at the next earnings report/call?

Speaking of GaN, if POWI pulls a rabbit out of a hat and does something unexpected during the next earnings release/call, then GaN may be at the center of it. POWI has a fab-lite business model, which entails using third-party foundries to do the actual manufacturing. Key partners are Lapis Semiconductor, Seiko Epson Corporation, and X-FAB.

The first two are based in Japan, which is also why a large amount of the silicon wafers supplied to POWI come from Japan. These foundries in turn rely on GaN substrates that are primarily supplied by Japanese companies like Sumitomo Chemical, Shin-Etsu Chemical, Mitsubishi Chemical, and others. However, the raw gallium is not from Japan, but almost exclusively sourced from China.

The issue here is that China has imposed restrictions on the export of gallium to Japan through export controls. This has greatly reduced supplies, although existing inventories have cushioned the impact. Gallium prices have still greatly increased, and it is possible POWI may be faced with cost increases as its suppliers need to pay a lot more than before, which could mean higher prices for customers like POWI.

This issue could affect profits and sales at POWI and the outlook by extension. If POWI sees some sort of impact because of the disruptions in the gallium market, then this could sway how the upcoming report is perceived by the market, especially since any adverse impact on growth would come at a bad time, so shortly after the spike in the stock price, and the higher growth expectations inherent to higher valuations.

Investor takeaways

Up until fairly recently, POWI was in a downtrend, a major obstacle to long POWI. But POWI was able to break the trend with a huge rally, particularly in the month of April, which saw the stock go vertical at one point. However, while the gains are impressive, an argument can be made that the foundation supporting the rally does not look all that sturdy, which is reason to doubt its sustainability.

The recent bidding up of the stock price seems to be based on the assumption growth will accelerate, but this is a risky assumption to make with no clear catalyst for this acceleration of growth to be found. POWI itself has not called for growth anywhere as fast. On the contrary, the current outlook calls for modest growth in FY2026, similar to FY2025.

POWI left open the possibility growth could be faster in the coming years, but that is not the same as affirming growth levels as indicated by some of the more optimistic growth estimates by some Wall Street firms. So even if growth does speed up, it is not guaranteed to hit a level fast enough to satisfy the much higher stock price.

Growth could even remain at the current levels, or slightly better, which is incompatible with the fast growth current valuations for POWI demand, including a P/E in the low fifties. This is also using non-GAAP, which leaves out a number of items. If GAAP is used and these items are included, then valuations are much higher.

While the spike in the stock price has pushed up valuations, the issue is that growth at POWI is not anywhere close to sufficient to warrant these elevated valuations. The most recent outlook for FY2026 only calls for modest growth, in the mid-single digits or so, although POWI did leave out the possibility that growth could get better down the road. The latter is something that will need to happen a lot of to justify the large increase in valuations.

The upcoming report on May 7 could shed light on whether POWI sees an acceleration in growth or not. At this time, there is little to suggest POWI will upgrade its growth outlook, let alone increase it to the extent needed to warrant the huge increase in the stock price. On the contrary, it is possible POWI may reveal disruptions in the supply chain involving GaN, which could impact upcoming quarterly results as a result of, for example, higher input costs and the impact that could have on margins and profits by extension.

It’s also worth pointing out that POWI has a history of having to revise its initial outlook to the downside. That was under the previous CEO, so it is possible the new CEO may have a better handle on this. Still, the fact remains that POWI cannot only not afford any slowdown in growth, but it also needs much faster growth than it has delivered in recent history. This looks difficult.

I am therefore neutral on POWI with this in mind. The stock has rallied, but there is little concrete at this time to support the rally. Growth is not growing fast enough to warrant paying the high multiples the stock goes for, nor is there reason to believe fast enough growth is around the corner, something even the current outlook for FY2026 from POWI agrees with.

There seems to be a major disconnect between what POWI has or is likely to deliver in terms of growth and what is required in terms of growth to support the recent increase in valuations. The risk is that this disconnect comes to the forefront at the next earnings report/call, which may trigger a reassessment of POWI after the spike in the stock price.

While there are some earnings projections calling for much faster earnings growth in FY2027, all current signs suggest this will be difficult to achieve. The most recent earnings call actually revealed that POWI itself is not totally sure of demand. Unless POWI radically revises its outlook at the next earnings call, and as things stand, the risk for POWI is therefore to the downside after the huge appreciation in the stock price, particularly in April.
